title: Proof-of-randomness protocol for blockchain consensus: a case of Macau algorithms
second_pass_summary: This paper proposes a blockchain consensus method called proof of randomness. Instead of making participants compete through heavy computation or coin ownership, each node uses true random numbers and hashing to help decide who can add to the chain. The authors argue that this could make block creation fairer and much less energy intensive than proof-of-work systems. They also frame the method as part of a new class of randomized algorithms, which they call Macau algorithms, and discuss possible links to quantum random-number hardware and quantum key distribution.

title: Careful Seeding for k-Medois Clustering with Incremental k-Means++ Initialization
second_pass_summary: This paper looks at a common weakness in k-medoids clustering: bad starting points can lead to poor final clusters. The authors propose a new incremental initialization method, INCKPP, that adds cluster centers one at a time using a k-means++ style strategy, so it is less sensitive to hand-tuned settings than earlier methods. They also introduce a faster version, INCKPPsample. Across synthetic and real datasets, including imbalanced ones, the proposed methods produce better clustering results, while the sampled version improves efficiency without giving up much accuracy.

title: Energy-efficient User Clustering for UAV-enabled Wireless Networks Using EM Algorithm
second_pass_summary: This paper studies how to group users and place drones in UAV-based wireless networks more efficiently. The authors propose a clustering method based on Gaussian mixture models and a modified expectation-maximization procedure, meant to serve as an initial deployment step before later optimization. The goal is to reduce power use and interference while keeping connections reliable. In their experiments, the method improved energy efficiency by about 25 percent and link reliability by about 18.3 percent compared with baseline approaches.

title: Analyzing Infrastructure LiDAR Placement with Realistic LiDAR Simulation Library
second_pass_summary: This paper studies where roadside LiDAR sensors should be placed so that vehicle-to-everything perception systems work better. The authors build a realistic LiDAR simulation library inside CARLA and use it to compare different installation layouts with multiple detection models. Their pipeline searches for stronger placements and then analyzes how point-cloud density and uniformity relate to perception quality. In the standard lane setting they report about a 15 percent gain in average precision over a conventional placement scheme, suggesting that better sensor placement alone can noticeably improve detection performance.

title: Discovering Structural Hole Spanners in Dynamic Networks via Graph Neural Networks
second_pass_summary: This paper focuses on finding structural hole spanners, the nodes that act as bridges between otherwise weakly connected groups in a changing network. Because finding the exact best set is computationally hard, the authors train a graph neural network to identify strong candidates across sequences of network snapshots. The method is designed for dynamic networks rather than fixed ones, which makes it closer to real social and information networks. In experiments, the proposed model was dramatically faster than the comparison method while still aiming to recover the most important bridge nodes.

title: Delaunay-like Triangulation of Smooth Orientable Submanifolds by L1-Norm Minimization
second_pass_summary: This is a geometry paper about reconstructing a smooth shape from a rough combinatorial approximation. The authors show that, under suitable conditions, the reconstruction problem can be rewritten as a weighted L1 optimization problem with linear constraints. That matters because it turns the triangulation task into something that can be solved with standard linear programming tools. They also prove that the solution is unique and matches a Delaunay-like triangulation studied in related work.

title: Decomposition horizons and a characterization of stable hereditary classes of graphs
second_pass_summary: This paper is a theoretical result about which hereditary graph classes count as stable. The authors connect ideas from graph decompositions and model theory, and show that stable hereditary graph classes are exactly the ones that admit quasibounded-size decompositions with bounded-shrubdepth base classes. In simpler terms, the paper gives a structural description of an important family of graphs. It also yields consequences about the kinds of large cliques or large independent sets graphs in these classes must contain.

title: Acceleration of Gossip Algorithms through the Euler-Poisson-Darboux Equation
second_pass_summary: This paper explains accelerated gossip algorithms by connecting them to continuous equations from mathematical physics. In the large-scale limit, the standard gossip method behaves like the heat equation, while an accelerated version behaves like the Euler-Poisson-Darboux equation, a damped wave equation. That difference helps explain why the accelerated method can spread information much faster across a network. The paper also includes simulations showing that these continuous models closely track the behavior of the original discrete algorithms.

title: Inferring Capabilities from Task Performance with Bayesian Triangulation
second_pass_summary: This paper proposes a Bayesian way to infer what capabilities a system likely has from how it performs across many tasks. Instead of relying on a single overall score, the method models how task features interact with hidden abilities and then estimates a richer cognitive profile. The authors demonstrate the approach on AnimalAI competitors and on agents tested with object-permanence tasks, showing that the resulting profiles are more informative and more predictive than simple benchmark averages. The main contribution is an evaluation framework for describing systems in terms of capabilities rather than just total performance.

title: Analyzing the Performance of ChatGPT in Cardiology and Vascular Pathologies
second_pass_summary: This study tests ChatGPT on 190 multiple-choice questions in cardiology and vascular pathology from the Siamois-QCM platform. ChatGPT answered 175 correctly, or 92.1 percent, which was higher than the scores of the two strong medical students used for comparison. The paper suggests that large language models could be useful as study aids in medical education, at least for question answering in this domain. At the same time, the authors note that more refinement and further evaluation are still needed before treating such systems as dependable domain-specific tools.

title: Revitalizing education through ict: a short overview of japan's current landscape
second_pass_summary: This paper gives a short overview of information and communication technology in Japanese education. Rather than proposing a new system, it reviews the main obstacles to broader ICT use, including cost, time, teaching conditions, teacher preparation, incentives, curriculum design, and health concerns. The goal is to highlight the practical barriers that shape how digital tools are adopted in Japan's education system.

title: Quantum Cross Subspace Alignment Codes via the $N$-sum Box Abstraction
second_pass_summary: This paper translates a family of coding methods used in classical communication into a quantum setting. Using an abstraction called the N-sum box, the authors build quantum cross-subspace alignment codes for quantum multiple-access channels and show how these codes can exploit entanglement efficiently. The work is motivated by applications such as private information retrieval and secure distributed matrix multiplication. In short, the paper provides a way to carry over useful alignment ideas from classical coding into quantum communication protocols.

title: Audio Contrastive-based Fine-tuning: Decoupling Representation Learning and Classification
second_pass_summary: This paper argues that fine-tuning an audio model should not mix representation learning and classifier training into one step. The authors propose a two-stage process: first refine the embedding space with contrastive tuning, then evaluate the learned representation with both a linear probe and a k-nearest-neighbors probe. This makes it easier to see whether the representation itself really improved. Across several audio classification tasks, the method outperformed standard fine-tuning and gave a clearer picture of why the learned features were better.

title: Spectral Norm, Economical Sieve, and Linear Invariance Testing of Boolean Functions
second_pass_summary: This paper studies how to test whether an unknown Boolean function is essentially the same as a known one after a linear change of coordinates, even when some errors are allowed. The main result is a much more query-efficient tolerant tester than previous work, along with a stronger lower bound showing that the problem still cannot be solved too cheaply. In practical terms, the paper narrows the gap between what is known to be possible and what is known to be necessary for this kind of invariance testing. It is mainly a theoretical advance in property testing and complexity.

title: Algorithms and Complexity for Computing Nash Equilibria in Adversarial Team Games
second_pass_summary: This paper studies adversarial team games, where several players with the same interests face a single opposing player in a zero-sum setting. The authors resolve the long-open complexity status of computing a standard Nash equilibrium by showing that the problem lies in the CLS class, which in turn gives CLS-completeness together with earlier hardness results. They also prove that an approximate stationary strategy for the team can be turned into an approximate equilibrium in polynomial time. Overall, the paper is a complexity-theoretic result that clarifies how hard these games are to solve.

title: Networked Communication for Decentralised Agents in Mean-Field Games
second_pass_summary: This paper brings networked communication into mean-field games for decentralized agents. The goal is to keep the scalability benefits of mean-field methods while relaxing the strong assumptions used by more classical approaches. The authors provide theory and empirical results showing that limited communication can substantially improve learning compared with fully independent agents, and can often approach the performance of more centralized methods. They also report gains in robustness when updates fail or when the population size changes.

title: Agile TLB Prefetching and Prediction Replacement Policy
second_pass_summary: This paper focuses on speeding up virtual-memory address translation, where TLB misses can trigger expensive page walks. The authors combine an Agile TLB Prefetcher with a prediction-based replacement policy to better anticipate which page-table entries will be needed and which cached entries are most useful to keep. The overall goal is to reduce translation overhead and improve memory-system performance beyond traditional replacement strategies such as LRU.

title: Predicting Company Growth using Scaling Theory informed Machine Learning
second_pass_summary: This paper tackles the problem of forecasting company growth by combining theory with machine learning. The proposed STIML framework uses a scaling-based model to capture the average structural growth trend and a data-driven model to predict the remaining fluctuations. Using Compustat data from more than 31,000 North American companies across many decades, the authors show that this hybrid approach improves prediction across multiple financial indicators and clarifies how trend and volatility contribute differently depending on company size.

title: EchoMimicV2: Towards Striking, Simplified, and Semi-Body Human Animation
second_pass_summary: This paper presents EchoMimicV2, a method for generating expressive half-body human animation from a reference image, an audio clip, and hand-pose input. The system is designed to reduce the number of cumbersome control conditions used by earlier methods while still improving facial motion, gestures, and overall visual quality. The authors report that the approach performs strongly in both quantitative and qualitative evaluations, making simplified yet high-quality human animation more practical.

title: MSM-BD: Multimodal Social Media Bot Detection Using Heterogeneous Information
second_pass_summary: This paper proposes MSM-BD, a multimodal system for detecting social-media bots. Instead of relying on only text or only user statistics, it combines heterogeneous information such as images, text, and account-level features to better distinguish automated accounts from human users. The motivation is that modern bots have become much more convincing, so stronger detection systems need to make use of multiple kinds of signals at once.

title: Precision or Peril: A PoC of Python Code Quality from Quantized Large Language Models
second_pass_summary: This study examines whether quantized language models can still produce good Python code while using fewer computational resources. The authors compare several open-source models, including 8-bit and 4-bit versions, on code-generation benchmarks and static quality checks. They find that smaller and quantized models can still generate usable code, but results are uneven and code quality problems remain, so the outputs should be carefully validated before real software use.

title: The Bright Side of Timed Opacity
second_pass_summary: Timed automata are mathematical models for systems where timing matters, and earlier work showed that checking opacity in these models is generally undecidable. This paper identifies several cases where the problem becomes manageable, including restrictions on the system or the attacker, and it studies the relationship between different notions of opacity. It also proposes new definitions that limit what an attacker can observe, leading to decidability results for the full timed-automata class under those bounded-observation settings.

title: Less is More Revisited: Association with Global Protocols and Multiparty Sessions
second_pass_summary: This paper revisits a long-running correctness issue in multiparty session types, a framework for checking whether distributed programs follow a communication protocol. Earlier work showed flaws in existing proofs that used end-point projection with mergeability, which raised doubts about soundness. The authors propose a new proof technique based on associating the behavior of a global protocol with its projected local types, and use it to recover soundness and key guarantees such as session fidelity, deadlock freedom, and liveness.

title: Parameterized Verification of Systems with Precise (0,1)-Counter Abstraction
second_pass_summary: This paper introduces a new way to verify systems that may contain any number of concurrent processes. The authors build a finite abstraction called a 01-counter system, which lets them decide several important verification questions such as reachability, coverability, and target problems for a broad class of well-structured systems. The framework also unifies and extends earlier results for models like broadcast networks, disjunctive systems, and systems with shared finite-domain variables.

title: POTSA: A Cross-Lingual Speech Alignment Framework for Speech-to-Text Translation
second_pass_summary: This paper introduces POTSA, a method for improving multilingual speech-to-text translation, especially for languages with less training data. The main idea is to align speech representations across languages so the model can better share useful semantic information instead of treating each source language too separately. The system first applies a coarse bias-compensation step, then uses Optimal Transport on parallel speech pairs to enforce finer alignment, and finally focuses that alignment on the layers where it helps most. On the FLEURS benchmark, the authors report stronger translation quality in both common and zero-shot languages. In practical terms, the work aims to reduce the performance gap between high-resource and low-resource speech translation settings.

title: A self-heating electrochemical cell with nine decades of programmable linear resistance
second_pass_summary: This paper introduces a new kind of programmable resistor for microelectronics. The device can be set to many stable resistance levels across an extremely large range while still keeping a nearly perfectly linear electrical response, which is important for accurate analog computation. The key idea is to use an electrochemical design that spreads heat and material changes through a larger volume instead of concentrating switching in a tiny weak spot. According to the paper, this greatly reduces conductance errors and allows thousands of usable states. The authors also show circuit-style operations such as amplification, division, and multiplication, suggesting the device could be useful for in-memory computing and other compact analog hardware.

title: Accurate Models of NVIDIA Tensor Cores
second_pass_summary: This paper builds highly accurate software models of NVIDIA tensor cores, the specialized hardware units that speed up matrix multiplication on GPUs. Because these units do not always behave like standard IEEE floating-point arithmetic, results can differ across GPU generations and can be hard to reproduce exactly. The authors reverse-engineer important numerical details such as rounding behavior, accumulator width, and normalization rules by designing sensitive test vectors and repeatedly comparing model predictions against real hardware outputs. They report bit-accurate models for several data-center GPUs and common low- or mixed-precision formats. These models can help researchers test numerical methods more reliably and study tensor-core behavior without always needing direct access to every target GPU.

title: A Modular Reference Architecture for MCP-Servers Enabling Agentic BIM Interaction
second_pass_summary: This paper proposes a modular reference architecture for MCP servers used in agentic BIM workflows. The motivation is that large language models are starting to interact with building information models through the Model Context Protocol, but many current BIM-side implementations are still ad hoc and tied to specific authoring tools. The authors design a microservice-style architecture with an explicit adapter layer so the MCP interface is separated from particular BIM APIs. They also build a prototype around IfcOpenShell and evaluate it on common model-generation and modification tasks. The main takeaway is not a new BIM agent itself, but a reusable systems design that could make LLM-driven BIM interaction more portable, reproducible, and easier to compare across tools.

title: Probabilistic algorithm for computing all local minimizers of Morse functions on a compact domain
second_pass_summary: This paper tackles the problem of finding every local minimum of a smooth function on a bounded region when the function can only be evaluated approximately. The authors design a probabilistic algorithm that combines polynomial approximation with algebraic system solving to return points whose small neighborhoods contain all local minimizers and keep them separated from one another. In other words, the method does not just find one good answer; it aims to find all of the locally best solutions. The paper also analyzes the algorithm's complexity under stated regularity assumptions and reports experiments showing that their Julia implementation can solve examples that earlier tools could not handle.

title: Next-Scale Prediction: A Self-Supervised Approach for Real-World Image Denoising
second_pass_summary: This paper proposes Next-Scale Prediction, a self-supervised method for removing noise from real photographs without needing clean ground-truth images for training. The central problem is that existing blind-spot methods must trade off between decorrelating noise and preserving fine visual detail. The authors try to break that trade-off by training the model across image scales: a low-resolution, more decorrelated input is used to predict a higher-resolution target that still contains important structure. The paper reports state-of-the-art results on real-world denoising benchmarks and notes that the same setup can also support super-resolution of noisy images without retraining. In simple terms, the method tries to clean images better while keeping edges and textures sharper.

title: When Agents are Powerful: Black Hole Search with Verification in Time-Varying Graphs
second_pass_summary: This paper studies a search problem on dynamic graphs where one node may act like a 'black hole' that destroys any agent entering it. The goal is to let a team of agents identify an edge touching that dangerous node while still ensuring at least one agent survives, or confirm that no black hole exists. Earlier work mainly assumed very limited communication between agents. Here, the authors strengthen the agents with one-hop visibility, global communication, or both, and show that these extra abilities lead to more efficient solutions. The main contribution is a set of improved algorithms and complexity results showing how better sensing or communication can sharply reduce the resources needed for reliable search in changing networks.

title: ARMOR: High-Performance Semi-Structured Pruning via Adaptive Matrix Factorization
second_pass_summary: This paper proposes ARMOR, a pruning method for large language models that tries to make them faster and smaller without hurting accuracy as much as standard sparse pruning. Instead of simply deleting weights, ARMOR factorizes each weight matrix into a sparse core plus two lightweight correction matrices that help repair the damage caused by pruning. The optimization is done in a one-shot post-training setting, so the method is meant to be practical after a model has already been trained. In experiments on Llama and Qwen models, the authors report better task performance and perplexity than earlier 2:4 semi-structured pruning methods while still keeping the hardware-friendly speed and memory benefits of sparse inference.

title: Automatically Generating Hard Math Problems from Hypothesis-Driven Error Analysis
second_pass_summary: This paper proposes an automated pipeline for generating harder math benchmark problems for large language models. Instead of only creating more examples from broad categories, the method first uses AI-generated hypotheses to identify the specific concepts or skills where a model seems error-prone, then generates new problems targeted at those weaknesses. The authors report that better hypotheses lead to harder generated questions, with the strongest set dropping Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct's accuracy well below its score on the original MATH benchmark. The broader point is that evaluation benchmarks need to evolve along with models. This system aims to create fresher and more diagnostic tests with much less manual effort than traditional benchmark construction.

title: BadgeX: IoT-Enhanced Wearable Analytics Meets LLMs for Collaborative Learning
second_pass_summary: This paper presents BadgeX, a wearable sensing system that combines smart badges or smartphones with large language models to analyze collaboration in real time. The system collects multimodal signals such as audio, images, motion, and depth, converts them into structured features, and then uses an LLM to turn those signals into higher-level narrative feedback grounded in learning theory. A pilot study suggests the platform can capture meaningful traces of group interaction and generate plausible descriptions of how people are collaborating. The larger goal is to make complex teamwork dynamics more visible in educational settings without requiring heavy manual observation, which could eventually support instructors or learners with faster feedback.

title: On the Fluctuations of the Single-Letter $d$-Tilted Sum for Binary Markov Sources
second_pass_summary: This paper analyzes a quantity from rate-distortion theory for binary Markov sources under Hamming distortion. Rather than focusing on a large operational object, the authors study the single-letter source-side d-tilted sum and show that, after centering, it can be written as a simple affine transform of the Markov chain's occupation count. That link lets them derive several exact results, including closed-form variance, the exact finite-length distribution, and a limiting cumulant generating function via a small transfer matrix. The contribution is mainly mathematical, but the payoff is clarity: the paper shows that the fluctuations of this information-theoretic quantity have a much simpler structure than they may first appear to have.

title: Comprehensive Analysis of Cellular Uplink Performance in a Dense Stadium Deployment
second_pass_summary: This paper measures 5G uplink performance in a large football stadium and shows that uploading data can remain a major bottleneck even in modern dense deployments. Using field measurements collected before and during a game, the authors find that high-frequency TDD bands struggle in the uplink because phones face strong propagation loss and receive relatively few uplink time slots. As a result, these bands may perform well for downloads but still provide weak upload capacity. The study concludes that lower-frequency FDD bands end up carrying much of the uplink burden. In plain terms, the paper shows why crowded venues can still have poor upload performance even when headline 5G downlink speeds look impressive.

title: Outlier-Robust Nonlinear Moving Horizon Estimation using Adaptive Loss Functions
second_pass_summary: This paper proposes a nonlinear moving horizon estimation method that is more robust to outliers. The core idea is to replace a fixed quadratic loss with an adaptive robust loss function, while adding regularization so the estimator does not fall into trivial solutions. A tuning parameter lets the method adjust how aggressively it downweights suspicious measurements. In simulations, the approach quickly adapts when outliers appear, but behaves more like standard L2 estimation when the data are clean. The main contribution is a practical estimation framework that can switch between accuracy and robustness more flexibly, which is useful in control and monitoring settings where occasional bad measurements can otherwise distort the state estimate.

title: Improving ML Attacks on LWE with Data Repetition and Stepwise Regression
second_pass_summary: This paper studies machine-learning attacks on the Learning With Errors problem, a core hardness assumption in lattice-based cryptography. Prior ML attacks worked best on relatively sparse secrets. The authors show that using larger datasets and repeated examples can push those attacks toward denser secrets, and they describe an empirical power-law relationship between recovery success, dataset size, and repetition. They also introduce a stepwise regression method meant to recover additional parts of the secret. The work does not break modern cryptography outright, but it does probe where some LWE variants may become more vulnerable, which is useful for stress-testing post-quantum systems and understanding the limits of ML-based cryptanalysis.

title: FAVE: Flow-based Average Velocity Establishment for Sequential Recommendation
second_pass_summary: This paper introduces FAVE, a recommendation method designed to generate the next item in a user sequence more efficiently than diffusion-style approaches. The authors argue that many flow-based recommenders still waste computation because they start from uninformative noise and spend effort modeling transitions that are partly deterministic. FAVE instead learns a direct one-step trajectory from a more informative starting point, using a two-stage training process that first builds a stable semantic space and then adds a semantic anchor prior plus a learned average velocity. The goal is to keep strong recommendation quality while reducing generation cost. In simple terms, the paper tries to make sequence recommendation both faster and more purposeful by starting closer to where the answer should be.

title: Subset Balancing and Generalized Subset Sum via Lattices
second_pass_summary: This paper studies the Subset Balancing problem and gives new algorithms based on lattice reductions. The key result is a reduction from subset balancing with coefficients in a bounded integer range to a single instance of an infinity-norm shortest vector problem. Using the best known lattice algorithms for that target problem, the authors obtain running times that depend on the dimension but no longer pay the usual log d cost in the exponent. For sufficiently large coefficient ranges, they also show the problem becomes polynomial-time solvable. The paper is mainly a complexity-theory advance, but the practical message is that a careful geometric reformulation can lead to substantially better algorithms than the classic meet-in-the-middle approach for many parameter settings.

title: CO^3: Cooperative Unsupervised 3D Representation Learning for Autonomous Driving
second_pass_summary: This paper proposes CO3, an unsupervised method for learning useful 3D representations from outdoor LiDAR data for autonomous driving. Instead of creating contrastive views from a single sensor alone, it uses cooperative views from vehicle-side and infrastructure-side LiDAR, and pairs that with a contextual shape-prediction task. The authors show that the learned representation transfers across different outdoor datasets and sensor types. On the ONCE and KITTI benchmarks, CO3 improves detection performance by up to 2.58 mAP over prior methods.

title: Space-efficient Data Structure for Next/Previous Larger/Smaller Value Queries
second_pass_summary: This paper designs a very compact data structure for answering several array queries, including range minimum and maximum queries and next or previous larger or smaller value queries, without needing to store the original array. The proposed encoding uses 3.701n plus lower-order bits and still supports efficient query times. The authors also prove a stronger lower bound, which helps show how close the new structure is to the best possible space usage. The result is mainly a theoretical advance in succinct data structures.

title: From Relevance to Utility: Evidence Retrieval with Feedback for Fact Verification
second_pass_summary: This paper studies how to retrieve better evidence for fact verification systems. The authors argue that evidence should be chosen not just because it looks relevant, but because it is actually useful for helping a verifier decide whether a claim is true or false. They propose a feedback-based retriever called FER that learns from the verifier's response, and experiments show that this improves both evidence selection and overall fact-checking performance.

title: ResidualPlanner+: a scalable matrix mechanism for marginals and beyond
second_pass_summary: This paper develops highly scalable matrix mechanisms for answering noisy marginal queries and related workloads under privacy constraints. ResidualPlanner is designed to be both scalable and optimal among Gaussian matrix mechanisms for marginals, while ResidualPlanner+ extends the approach to more general workloads such as mixtures of marginals and range or prefix-sum queries. The authors report large speed and scalability gains over prior methods, and show that ResidualPlanner+ can also outperform the previous state of the art on prefix-sum workloads. The main contribution is making these privacy-preserving query planners practical at much larger scales.

title: PlayFutures: Imagining Civic Futures with AI and Puppets
second_pass_summary: This paper presents PlayFutures, a workshop for children around ages 9 to 12 that combines AI image generation, puppet making, and storytelling. The idea is to help children imagine future versions of civic spaces, reflect on how AI shapes those futures, and discuss public life through play and performance. The work treats children as important participants in thinking about technology and cities, even though they are often left out of formal civic decision-making.

title: TT-SNN: Tensor Train Decomposition for Efficient Spiking Neural Network Training
second_pass_summary: This paper tries to make spiking neural networks cheaper to train. The authors introduce TT-SNN, which uses tensor-train decomposition to compress network weights, lowering memory use, computation, and latency. They also add a parallel computation pipeline and report strong results on static and dynamic vision benchmarks, suggesting that the method can make these energy-efficient models more practical.

title: ShadowNPU: System and Algorithm Co-design for NPU-Centric On-Device LLM Inference
second_pass_summary: This paper addresses a bottleneck in running large language models on mobile devices. Current frameworks often send the attention operation from the NPU back to the CPU or GPU because of quantization issues, which hurts speed and efficiency. The authors propose shadowAttn, a sparse attention design that keeps more of the work on the NPU through techniques such as pilot computation, compute-graph bucketing, and head-wise pipelining, aiming to preserve accuracy while reducing dependence on CPU and GPU resources.

title: Parameterized complexity of the f-Critical Set problem
second_pass_summary: This paper studies a graph problem about choosing a small starting set of vertices that causes an entire network to flip into a stable all-ones state after one update step. The authors analyze how hard this 'f-Critical Set' problem is under different parameter settings. They prove that the problem stays computationally hard in several restricted cases, including some planar graph families, but also identify settings where it becomes fixed-parameter tractable. In addition, they provide two kernelization results that compress an instance down to a smaller equivalent one based on key parameters. The paper's main value is theoretical: it maps out where the problem is likely intractable and where more efficient parameterized algorithms are possible.

title: Paper Espresso: From Paper Overload to Research Insight
second_pass_summary: This paper presents Paper Espresso, an open-source platform for tracking and summarizing trends in arXiv papers. The system automatically finds new papers, generates structured summaries with topical labels and keywords, and then performs trend analysis over daily, weekly, and monthly time scales. Over almost three years of deployment, the platform processed more than 13,000 papers and used those summaries to surface larger patterns in AI research, including shifts in popular topics and links between novelty and community interest. The contribution is partly a tool and partly a data resource: the authors show that automated large-scale summarization can help researchers navigate paper overload and study how a field is changing over time.

title: Healthcare App Design in Low-Resource Contexts: Challenges, Practices, and Opportunities
second_pass_summary: This paper discusses why healthcare apps designed for well-resourced settings often fail in low-resource contexts. The authors emphasize that many deployments must work around unstable infrastructure, limited digital literacy, language diversity, and reliance on community health workers or informal care networks. Rather than proposing a single technical model, the paper frames a research and design agenda for building digital health tools that better match local realities. It highlights the need to consider culture, usability, and institutional constraints from the start. In short, the piece argues that effective healthcare technology for underserved settings requires context-sensitive design, not just scaling down apps that were built for very different environments.
